perturbation.original_text: 8) This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of California and the laws of Hong Kong. In the event of a dispute, the parties shall seek mediation at a third country mutually agreed upon.
perturbation.changed_text: 8) This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of Hong Kong. In the event of a dispute, the parties shall seek resolution through binding arbitration in Hong Kong.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the application of California law and mandates binding arbitration in Hong Kong. By eliminating California law as a governing option, the contract denies the sponsor certain protections available under California consumer protection laws, if the SLD can be considered a consumer.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) in California provides specific protections against unfair or deceptive acts. By removing California law as a governing law, the agreement attempts to bypass these consumer protections, potentially violating the CLRA if the services or goods were intended for personal, family, or household use and the Sponsor qualifies as a consumer. This is especially relevant if the Sponsor is considered a consumer based on its use of the sponsorship for reaching the general public.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1750. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes California law as a governing law, which contradicts the Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) as explained. The law explanation states that by removing California law, the agreement attempts to bypass consumer protections provided by the CLRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies CIV 1750 as part of the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. The omission of California law directly removes the possibility of invoking CLRA protections, creating a clear conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 5) ACM retains all of its rights under copyright and trademark laws pertaining to the Event's intellectual property, whether registered or unregistered, and any applications of the Event's logo, name, characters and likeness. Video and audio excerpts of the Event must have ACM's approval in writing prior to such use. The Sponsor shall retain all of its rights under copyright and trademark laws pertaining to any of its intellectual property.
perturbation.changed_text: 5) ACM retains all of its rights under copyright and trademark laws pertaining to the Event's intellectual property. Video and audio excerpts of the Event must have ACM's approval prior to such use. The Sponsor shall retain all of its rights under copyright and trademark laws pertaining to any of its intellectual property.
perturbation.explanation: This change omits the phrase "whether registered or unregistered, and any applications of the Event's logo, name, characters and likeness" when mentioning ACM's IP rights. While ACM still retains its rights, by omitting the phrase, it becomes ambiguous what specific IP rights ACM is referring to. This may cause legal challenges for the sponsor, when they wish to use any materials from the event, thinking they aren't violating any registered IP of ACM, but still get sued. By making the terms of which IP is protected unclear, ACM does not meet requirements of reasonable disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code - Duty of Disclosure
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including unfair or deceptive business practices. Although this change in the language does not explicitly violate trademark laws, it creates an ambiguity that ACM can exploit, making the agreement potentially unfair to the Sponsor. This omission could be considered an unfair business practice by obscuring the true scope of ACM's intellectual property rights, leading to potential disputes or unknowingly infringement from the sponsor.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract creates ambiguity regarding ACM's intellectual property rights by omitting the phrase "whether registered or unregistered, and any applications of the Event's logo, name, characters and likeness." This ambiguity potentially violates California Business and Professions Code § 17200, which prohibits unfair competition, including unfair or deceptive business practices. As the law snippet states, "unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising." The omission could be considered an unfair business practice as it obscures the full scope of ACM's IP rights, leading to potential disputes or unintentional infringement by the sponsor. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
